_set_app is actually a py.test fixture (I'm using the pytext.fixture decorator, as a function). With ``autouse=True``, I tell py.test to run this fixture for every test in this class. It is getting the ``app`` in the first place - and this is where py.test's magic hits - simply because it has an argument named ``app`` and there is an existing fixture named ``app`` (in ``sphinx.testing.fixtures``). And after the ``yield`` statement the test is over and we cleanup the mess we've done :).

If you define ``test_foo`` and ``test_bar`` methods, the sphinx project will indeed be launched and initialized twice, with the exact same configuration options (I'd want to change that, probably using a basic custom decorator and/or detect a previous use of @pytest.mark.sphinx in the metaclass, but for the moment the aim was to exactly understand and highlight how it worked!).

My only concern with this pytest.fixture approach for the moment is we can't just run the configuration stage (i.e. just call ``configs.apply_sphinx_configurations``) to test how exhale extracts its configuration without generating the rst files on a project. But I'm quite sure it'll be possible to do that with a bit more digging.

Thanks to your efforts it's definitely cleaner now than my first approach with ``configure`` and ``run_exhale`` though!

Hm, I believe we should explicitly set an encoding. If I'm not mistaken by default will be used encoding extracted from current locale, but OpenAPI spec may use another encoding.

So I can think about two options:

* Explicitly use `UTF-8` and say that only `UTF-8` specs are supported. At least we will know for sure which encoding to use to deal with files no matter which system we're running on.

* Use encoding from Sphinx config: `env.config.source_encoding`, and say that the specs must have the same encoding as other Sphinx documents.

* Read encoding value from per-spec configuration.

While third item is more flexible, it would require additional changes and thorough thinking. I'd suggest to go with either (1) or (2) in this PR.

I thought about that option, but the following reasons made me not taking that route:
1. `get_active_runs` is actually inconsistent - the name does not reflect what it does, hence it needs to be change; additionally, it's not very useful as it only return execution_dates instead of dag run objects to be more generic and actually use for this purpose as well.
2. Extending `get_active_runs` a little bit would require more thought since the implementation seems very particular to a use case, again, returning only the execution_dates. Thus, this will broad the scope of the PR, which we're trying to avoid.
3. Even though performance seems not to be an issue in this case, having a query that only fetched the numeric count would reduce the number of bits going through the pipe, so that's always good. There are many cases where we don't need the full payload of all active runs, specially when calling the count repeatedly.

Hence, I decided to go agains using `get_active_runs` and implement a more sensitive function: `get_num_active_runs` (renamed after suggestion by @aoen) - Also, seems like this same query could be used in some other parts of the code base, but I think for now to avoid further changes in other places we can avoid them.

`roles/owner` and `roles/pubsub.admin` are not equivalent, and I don't see why they should be bundled into one set. I think this is a bug not a feature. `roles/owner` applies only on projects, and simply cascades down to resources in the project namespace. It can't actually be set on a pubsub policy. It's a legacy role with a much too wide set of permissions, and should be avoided if at all possible. It's much better to be explicit, and only interact with `policy.admins`. Then `owners` can be a frozenset to avoid accidental modification, which will result in an API error.

Yeah, it's not ideal. Thing is, that abstraction will be a bit broken by *any* mechanism that allows goal-level options, because something has to register them once at the goal level, and then each task has to know to *use* the option. So in some sense the task knows its goal, or at least it knows it's in some goal that has those options registered.

Using a subsystem would require changing the names of all those task-level options, which not only imposes a massive deprecation cycle, but also makes the names clunkier. (I don't think we can register a subsystem with the same scope as a goal, that is asking for all kinds of trouble). I kinda like that this is lightweight, and just leverages our existing ability to register options recursively. 

So how about this - instead of the mixin specifying the goal it's in, it specifies the options it has access to. I.e., we get rid of `goal_options_registrar_cls`? That's really only needed for test setup anyway, and we can work around that some other way. So now there's no link between the task and its goal name, so you can register the task in multiple goals (as long as the goal provides the options, of course)?

IMO the migration process would benefit from using custom commands instead of purely reflecting on the SDK.  This approach requires some kind of guide to really understand the process and sequence the necessary calls.  Ideally, I could do just the following:
`az servicebus migration start …` and it would start the migration, poll until it was done, the complete the process (switch the connection strings) and clean up (delete) the migration object.  From our meeting, that's probably too much than you'd like, but you could also reduce this to two commands:

```
az servicebus migration start …  # start migration and poll until done
az servicebus migration complete --clean-up  # complete migration and clean up the migration object
```
This would be a more convenient 2-step solution to the current process:
```
az servicebus migration start
az servicebus migration show # as many times as needed until done
az servicebus migration complete
az servicebus migration delete
```

For this, you would make "start" and complete custom commands.

```Python
def start_servicebus_migration(cmd, client, …, no_wait=False):
  if no_wait:
    # This is effectively what the current command would do...
    return client.create_and_start_migration(…)

  client.create_and_start_migration(…)

  # pseudocode--obviously don't poll infinitely and handle exceptions :)
  while(True):
    time.sleep(30) # some delay
    result = client.get(…)
    if result.code == 200:
      # this lets the user know what to do next. Atypical, but this is a one-time
      # interactive command so this kind of this is acceptable
      logger.warning('Migration to Premium namespace is finished. Run `az servicebus migration complete` to switch connection strings and complete the migration process.'
      return
```

Then complete just looks like:
```Python
def complete_migration(cmd, client, …, clean_up=False):
  client.complete_migration(…)

  # good reminder for what connection string references which namespace
  # helps clarify the confusing "post-migration-name" parameter.
  logger.warning('Servicebus namespace migration complete. Use the following connection strings to access your namespaces:)
  logger.warning('\tStandard: %s', standard_cs)
  logger.warning('\tPremium: %s', premium_cs)
  if clean_up:
    client.delete(…)
  return 
```

You are correct in observing that this is done in other parts of the library. You are also correct that both versions are effectively the same; however, there is a difference. `self` refers directly to the instance of `CryptographicParameters` (which may vary from a vanilla instance depending on what's done with it by the instantiating code), while specifying `CryptographicParameters.` is a direct reference to the class definition (which is always what we want here). This actually ties into Python metaclass mechanics and how attributes are handled and accessed but that's a deep rabbit hole. To quote the Zen of Python, "Explicit is better than implicit."

I used to not really care about explicit vs implicit references. However, this ended up causing a nasty self reflection bug way back in an earlier version of the library (two years ago or so). Now, I try to avoid overusing `self`. One of my (many) TODOs is to go through and fix these references to explicitly use class names; I've just never gotten around to it. I also plan on pulling out these internal class definitions (e.g., `BlockCipherMode`, `PaddingMethod`, etc) and placing them in the outer module scope. Again, one of my many TODOs.

In my experience, class attributes are deceptive. They seem simple when you start using them, but become a real pain down the road when more use cases for your class arise. Here's two specific issues:

* Let's say a user installs Pulp Smash for the very first time and executes `make docs-html`. As part of the documentation generation process, this module is imported and `get_config` is called. Since the user has just installed Pulp Smash, a config will not be found, and a `ConfigFileNotFoundError` is raised. :cry:
* Let's say we have two Pulp servers, and we want to monitor a task on each of them. It would be nice if we could instantiate two `Task` objects, give each a unique `cfg` object and call `wait_for_task()` on each. Using a class attribute makes this impossible.

As we discussed before, this thread is not CPU-bounded. The task performed in the thread is downloading docker image, which is mostly network-bounded not cpu-bounded. There's a lot of potential for deadlock here. In general there should be no blocking in the resource monitor that can't be canceled by closing `rm.cancel`. gRPC is going to wait for all in-flight RPCs to drain before shutting down the server and the `defaultProviders` server is going to exit if it gets canceled mid-operation.

We should either be returning a channel here that we `select` over `with <-rm.cancel` like we do on line 539, so we can report that the resource monitor is shutting down, or we should return some sort of well-known error that indicates that the resmon is shutting down.
